HLD learning path

Common product design patterns

Practice recurring architectures that appear across many interview prompts and real-world products.

Module progress 0%

0 of 5 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Feeds and timelines

Feeds combine ranking, fan-out, caching, and read-time assembly decisions under heavy skewed traffic.

Why this module matters Build one clean mental model before scaling it.

Move through the lessons in order so each trade-off has context before the harder distributed systems decisions show up.

Study target 5 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

High-level design interview prep 5 lessons 0/5 complete Follow in order for best results Back to HLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the summary, inspect the diagram, and identify the sharpest trade-off.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
HLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Recognize reusable patterns behind common product families
  • Explain fan-out, indexing, storage, and notification decisions
  • Use these patterns as building blocks in case studies

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

Chat, presence, and notifications

Payments and ledger-style systems

Money movement demands idempotency, auditability, reconciliation, and a conservative approach to asynchronous side effects.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Feeds and timelines

Open

Feeds combine ranking, fan-out, caching, and read-time assembly decisions under heavy skewed traffic.

Why it matters

This pattern shows up in social, commerce, news, and activity products, making it a high-yield interview topic.

What you will cover

Read-time vs. write-time fan-outRanking and retrieval pipelineCorrectness and product controls

Good interview signal

  • State whether the design fans out on read, write, or both.
  • Separate event storage from derived feed views.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

File upload, processing, and distribution

Open

Media-heavy products require a pipeline for uploads, validation, durable storage, transformation, and fast global delivery.

Why it matters

This pattern combines object storage, asynchronous work, metadata stores, and CDN behavior in a concrete way.

What you will cover

Upload flowProcessing pipelineServing and lifecycle

Good interview signal

  • Use direct uploads to object storage when files are large.
  • Process heavy transformations asynchronously.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Search and autocomplete

Open

Search systems combine ingestion, indexing, ranking, caching, and query-time latency controls.

Why it matters

Autocomplete is a strong interview prompt because it requires you to reason about prefix retrieval, freshness, and hot terms.

What you will cover

Separate source of truth from search indexAutocomplete data structuresLatency and relevance

Good interview signal

  • Keep search indexes derived from the source of truth.
  • Choose an autocomplete structure based on update and query shape.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 4

Chat, presence, and notifications

Open

Messaging products blend low-latency delivery, persistent history, fan-out, device state, and notification fallbacks.

Why it matters

This pattern is useful because it combines real-time delivery with offline durability and multi-device consistency.

What you will cover

Separate message durability from delivery statePresence and connection managementNotification fan-out

Good interview signal

  • Persist messages before treating them as sent.
  • Model presence as soft state separate from history.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 5

Payments and ledger-style systems

Open

Money movement demands idempotency, auditability, reconciliation, and a conservative approach to asynchronous side effects.

Why it matters

Even if you never design a payments product in production, this lesson forces precise thinking about correctness under failure.

What you will cover

Double-entry and immutable eventsSynchronous correctness, asynchronous integrationsOperational safeguards

Good interview signal

  • Use immutable ledger events or equivalent audit records.
  • Make transaction creation idempotent.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
